name: Oregon Dept of Administrative Services (DAS/EIS)
tagline: Oregon's central services agency: DAS runs statewide administration and, through its Enterprise Information Services (EIS) division under the State CIO, owns enterprise IT, data centers, cybersecurity, and the AI and cloud policy gates that shape how the state buys technology.
overview: The Oregon Department of Administrative Services (DAS) is the state's central administrative agency — it provides shared business services (HR, finance, fleet, facilities, procurement) to other agencies and sets statewide operating policy. For a technology seller, the relevant engine inside DAS is Enterprise Information Services (EIS), the division led by State Chief Information Officer Terrence Woods that owns statewide IT strategy, the State Data Center, enterprise security, and emerging AI governance. EIS sets the policies and standards that other Oregon agencies follow, runs shared infrastructure, and is the office shaping how the state approaches generative AI and cloud. DAS Procurement Services operates the statewide contracting vehicles — including Statewide Price Agreements and the OregonBuys eProcurement platform — that most state agencies, and many local governments through cooperative purchasing, buy technology through. DAS is best understood as both a policy-and-standards authority (via EIS) and the central path to market (via Procurement Services) rather than the largest end spender of IT dollars.

budget.intro: DAS is funded largely through assessments and charges to the agencies it serves rather than a single large general-fund IT line, so its leverage is in the standards it sets and the contracts that run through it. Oregon budgets on a two-year cycle; the 2025–27 biennial budget is the relevant planning window, and enterprise IT investments are reviewed through the state's IT governance and stage-gate process.

stats.label: Current biennium
stats.note: Oregon's two-year budget cycle; the planning window for enterprise IT

stats.label: Enterprise IT owner
stats.note: DAS division under the State CIO; statewide IT, data center, security

stats.label: IT investment review
stats.note: Major IT projects reviewed via state IT governance / oversight

notes: DAS recovers much of its cost through assessments and service charges to client agencies, not a large standalone general-revenue appropriation — confirm exact figures against the enacted 2025–27 budget before forecasting.
The largest end IT spend sits with program agencies (e.g., human services, transportation, revenue); EIS sets the standards and shared infrastructure those agencies build on.

initiatives.name: State AI Action Plan & AI governance
initiatives.summary: Oregon has moved to govern state use of generative AI through an AI advisory body and action plan coordinated through DAS/EIS. Expect procurement guardrails, acceptable-use policy, and pilots — align AI-infrastructure and trusted-AI messaging to the state's own framework.

initiatives.name: Cloud security gate (GovRAMP)
initiatives.summary: Oregon is among states adopting GovRAMP (formerly StateRAMP) as a cloud-security authorization standard. Cloud and as-a-service offerings should hold the appropriate authorization level to clear procurement — treat it as an early gate, not an afterthought.

initiatives.name: State Data Center & infrastructure modernization
initiatives.summary: EIS operates Oregon's enterprise data center and shared infrastructure. Ongoing refresh, hybrid-cloud, and resiliency work is a recurring track for ISG infrastructure, storage, and managed services.

initiatives.name: Enterprise cybersecurity & Cyber Oregon
initiatives.summary: EIS houses enterprise security and the state CISO function, with statewide standards and incident response. Security modernization and zero-trust alignment are durable themes across state and, via shared services, local entities.

procurement.intro: Selling to Oregon state agencies runs through DAS Procurement Services and its statewide vehicles rather than one-off agency RFPs. Confirm two things first: that Dell holds (or can ride) the right Statewide Price Agreement and is set up in OregonBuys, and that any cloud/as-a-service offering can meet Oregon's cloud-security authorization expectations.

vehicles.name: Statewide Price Agreements (SPAs)
vehicles.detail: DAS-managed contracts that state agencies use to buy without running their own solicitation — the primary path for technology and equipment.

vehicles.name: OregonBuys
vehicles.detail: Oregon's statewide eProcurement platform; vendors must be registered and contracts are surfaced/transacted here.

vehicles.name: ORCPP (Oregon Cooperative Procurement Program)
vehicles.detail: Lets eligible local governments and other public entities buy off state contracts — extends reach beyond state agencies.

powerStructure.name: DAS Director
powerStructure.title: Director, Dept of Administrative Services
powerStructure.note: Leads the central services agency; sets enterprise operating direction. Confirm the current named director before outreach.

powerStructure.name: Terrence Woods
powerStructure.title: State Chief Information Officer (DAS/EIS)
powerStructure.note: Owns statewide IT strategy, the State Data Center, security, and AI governance — the top technology relationship for the DAS/EIS motion.

powerStructure.name: State Chief Data Officer
powerStructure.title: Enterprise data strategy
powerStructure.note: Owns data governance, sharing, and analytics direction — relevant for data-platform and AI-readiness conversations. Confirm current officeholder.

powerStructure.name: State Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)
powerStructure.title: Enterprise security & standards
powerStructure.note: Owns statewide security policy, GovRAMP posture, and incident response — the gate for anything touching state data.

powerStructure.name: DAS Procurement Services
powerStructure.title: Statewide contracting & OregonBuys
powerStructure.note: Owns SPAs, solicitations, and vendor onboarding — the path onto the vehicles agencies buy through.

news.title: Oregon advances State AI Action Plan and AI governance through DAS/EIS
news.impact: AI acceptable-use, procurement guardrails, and pilots are taking shape — align trusted-AI and AI-infrastructure messaging to the state's framework.

news.title: Oregon among states adopting GovRAMP for cloud-service authorization
news.impact: Cloud/as-a-service deals should clear the appropriate authorization level early to stay sellable to state agencies.

news.title: EIS continues enterprise data-center and cybersecurity modernization under State CIO Terrence Woods
news.impact: Stable State CIO leadership keeps infrastructure refresh and security as durable buying tracks.

dellActions: Confirm Dell's Statewide Price Agreement coverage and OregonBuys registration — this is the access path to Oregon state agencies and, via ORCPP, many local entities.
Lead with ISG infrastructure, storage, and managed services tied to EIS's State Data Center and hybrid-cloud modernization.
Map the AI conversation to Oregon's State AI Action Plan — position trusted-AI infrastructure that fits the state's own governance, not around it.
Clear Oregon's cloud-security expectations (GovRAMP) early for any cloud / as-a-service play that touches state data.
Build the State CIO (Terrence Woods) and CISO relationships as the policy-and-standards owners, then extend into program agencies that follow EIS standards.
